content:
# Exodus Chapter 23 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~45v–46r (PDF pp. 89–90); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 65–66 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_023_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_023_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_023_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_023_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Laws Concerning Justice and Truthfulness (vv. 1–3)

**1** You shall not raise [**lo tissa**] a false [**shav**] report [**shema**]. Do not put [**al-tashet**] your hand [**yadekha**] with the wicked [**im-rasha**] to be [**lihyot**] a malicious [**chamas**] witness [**ed**].

**2** You shall not follow [**lo-tihyeh**] after the many [**acharey-rabbim**] for evil [**lera'ot**], and you shall not testify [**velo-ta'aneh**] concerning a dispute [**al-riv**] to turn aside [**lintot**] after the many [**acharey rabbim**] to pervert justice [**lehattot**].

**3** And a poor man [**vedal**] you shall not favor [**lo tehdar**] in his dispute [**berivo**].

---

## Laws Concerning the Enemy's Property (vv. 4–5)

**4** When you encounter [**ki tifga**] the ox [**shor**] of your enemy [**oyivkha**] or his donkey [**o chamoro**] going astray [**to'eh**], you shall surely return it [**hashev teshivennu**] to him [**lo**].

**5** When you see [**ki-tir'eh**] the donkey [**chamor**] of one who hates you [**sona'akha**] lying down [**rovets**] under [**tachat**] its burden [**masa'o**], you shall refrain [**vechadalta**] from leaving it [**me'azov**] to him [**lo**]; you shall surely release it [**azov ta'azov**] with him [**immo**].

---

## Laws Concerning Justice in Court (vv. 6–9)

**6** You shall not pervert [**lo tatteh**] the justice [**mishpat**] of your poor [**evyonekha**] in his dispute [**berivo**].

**7** From a false [**mishsheqer**] matter [**middevar**] keep far away [**tirchaq**], and the innocent [**venaqqi**] and the righteous [**vetsaddiq**] do not kill [**al-taharog**], for I will not justify [**ki lo-atsdiq**] the wicked [**rasha**].

**8** And a bribe [**veshochad**] you shall not take [**lo tiqqach**], for the bribe [**ki hashshochad**] blinds [**ye'aver**] the clear-sighted [**piqqechim**] and perverts [**visalef**] the words [**divrey**] of the righteous [**tsaddiqim**].

**9** And a sojourner [**veger**] you shall not oppress [**lo tilchats**], for you [**ve'attem**] know [**yeda'tem**] the soul [**et-nefesh**] of the sojourner [**haggar**], for sojourners [**ki-gerim**] you were [**heyitem**] in the land of [**be'erets**] Egypt [**Mitsrayim**].

---

## The Sabbath Year and the Sabbath Day (vv. 10–13)

**10** And six years [**veshesh shanim**] you shall sow [**tizra**] your land [**et-artsekha**] and you shall gather in [**ve'asafta**] its produce [**et-tevu'atah**].

**11** But the seventh year [**vehashevi'it**] you shall let it rest [**tishmetenah**] and let it lie fallow [**untashtah**], and the poor [**evyoney**] of your people [**ammekha**] shall eat [**ve'akhlu**], and what they leave over [**veyitram**] the beasts [**chayyat**] of the field [**hassadeh**] shall eat [**tokhal**]. So [**ken**] you shall do [**ta'aseh**] with your vineyard [**lekharmekha**] and with your olive grove [**lezeyitekha**].

**12** Six days [**sheshet yamim**] you shall do [**ta'aseh**] your work [**ma'asekha**], and on the seventh day [**uvayyom hashevi'i**] you shall rest [**tishbot**], in order that [**lema'an**] your ox [**shorekha**] and your donkey [**vachamorekha**] may rest [**yanuach**], and the son of [**ven**] your female servant [**amatekha**] and the sojourner [**vehagger**] may be refreshed [**veyinnafesh**].

**13** And in all [**uvekhol**] that I have said [**asher-amarti**] to you [**aleykhem**], take heed [**tishameru**]. And the name [**veshem**] of other [**acherim**] gods [**elohim**] you shall not mention [**lo tazkiru**]; it shall not be heard [**lo yishama**] upon [**al**] your mouth [**pikha**].

---

## The Three Pilgrimage Festivals (vv. 14–19)

**14** Three times [**shalosh regalim**] you shall keep a feast [**tachog**] to Me [**li**] in the year [**bashanah**].

**15** The Feast of Unleavened Bread [**et-chag hamatstsot**] you shall keep [**tishmor**]: seven days [**shiv'at yamim**] you shall eat [**tokhal**] unleavened bread [**matstsot**], as [**ka'asher**] I commanded you [**tsivvitikha**], at the appointed time [**lemo'ed**] of the month of [**chodesh**] Abib [**ha'Aviv**], for in it [**ki-vo**] you came out [**yatsata**] from Egypt [**miMitsrayim**]. And none shall appear [**velo-yera'u**] before My face [**panay**] empty-handed [**reyqam**].

**16** And the Feast of Harvest [**vechag haqqatsir**], the firstfruits [**bikkurey**] of your labors [**ma'asekha**] which [**asher**] you sow [**tizra**] in the field [**bassadeh**]; and the Feast of Ingathering [**vechag ha'asif**] at the going out [**betset**] of the year [**hashanah**], when you gather in [**be'ospekha**] your labors [**et-ma'asekha**] from [**min**] the field [**hassadeh**].

**17** Three times [**shalosh pa'amim**] in the year [**bashanah**] all [**kol**] your males [**zekhuurekha**] shall appear [**yera'eh**] before [**el-peney**] the Lord [**ha'Adon**] YHWH [**YHWH**].

**18** You shall not offer [**lo-tizbach**] the blood [**dam**] of My sacrifice [**zivchi**] with [**al**] leavened bread [**chamets**], and the fat [**vechelev**] of My feast [**chaggi**] shall not remain overnight [**lo-yalin**] until [**ad**] morning [**boqer**].

**19** The best [**re'shit**] of the firstfruits [**bikkurey**] of your ground [**admatekha**] you shall bring [**tavi**] to the house [**beyt**] of YHWH [**YHWH**] your God [**Elohekha**]. You shall not boil [**lo-tevashel**] a kid [**gedi**] in the milk of [**bachalev**] its mother [**immo**].

---

## The Angel of YHWH and the Conquest Promises (vv. 20–33)

**20** Behold [**hinneh**], I am sending [**anokhi shole'ach**] an angel [**mal'akh**] before you [**lefanekha**] to guard you [**lishmorrekha**] on the way [**badderekh**] and to bring you [**ulhavi'akha**] to [**el**] the place [**hamaqom**] which [**asher**] I have prepared [**hakhinoti**].

**21** Take heed [**hishamer**] before him [**mippanav**] and listen [**ushema**] to his voice [**beqolo**]; do not rebel [**al-tammer**] against him [**bo**], for he will not forgive [**ki lo yissa**] your transgression [**lefish'akhem**], for My name [**ki shemi**] is in his midst [**beqirbo**].

**22** But if [**ki im**] you indeed listen [**shamoa tishma**] to his voice [**beqolo**] and do [**ve'asita**] all [**kol**] that [**asher**] I speak [**adabber**], then I will be an enemy [**ve'ayyavti**] to your enemies [**et-oyevekha**] and an adversary [**vetsarti**] to your adversaries [**et-tsaraykha**].

**23** For My angel [**ki-yelekh mal'akhi**] shall go [**yelekh**] before you [**lefanekha**] and bring you [**vehevi'akha**] to [**el**] the Amorite [**ha'Emori**] and the Hittite [**vehaChitti**] and the Perizzite [**vehaPerizzi**] and the Canaanite [**vehaKena'ani**] and the Hivite [**haChivvi**] and the Jebusite [**vehaYevusi**], and I will cut them off [**vehikhadettiv**].

**24** You shall not bow down [**lo-tishtachaveh**] to their gods [**leloheyhem**] and you shall not serve them [**velo to'ovdem**], and you shall not do [**velo ta'aseh**] according to their deeds [**kema'aseyhem**], but you shall utterly tear them down [**ki haros teharresem**] and you shall utterly break [**veshabber teshabber**] their pillars [**matstsevoteyhem**].

**25** And you shall serve [**va'avadtem**] YHWH [**et-YHWH**] your God [**Eloheykhem**], and He will bless [**uverakh**] your bread [**et-lachmekha**] and [**ve'et**] your water [**meymekha**], and I will remove [**vahasiroti**] sickness [**machalah**] from your midst [**miqqirbekha**].

**26** There shall not be [**lo tihyeh**] a woman who miscarries [**meshakkelah**] or is barren [**va'aqarah**] in your land [**be'artsekha**]; the number [**mispar**] of your days [**yamekha**] I will fulfill [**amalle**].

**27** My terror [**et-eymati**] I will send [**ashalach**] before you [**lefanekha**], and I will throw into confusion [**vehammoti**] all [**et-kol**] the people [**ha'am**] among whom [**asher**] you come [**tavo bahem**], and I will give [**venatatti**] all [**et-kol**] your enemies [**oyevekha**] to you [**eleykha**] as those who turn the back [**oref**].

**28** And I will send [**veshalachti**] the hornet [**et-hatsir'ah**] before you [**lefanekha**], and it shall drive out [**vegershah**] the Hivite [**et-haChivvi**], the Canaanite [**et-haKena'ani**], and the Hittite [**ve'et-haChitti**] from before you [**millefanekha**].

**29** I will not drive them out [**lo agareshennu**] from before you [**mippanekha**] in one year [**beshanah echat**], lest [**pen**] the land [**ha'arets**] become [**tihyeh**] desolate [**shemamah**] and the beasts [**chayyat**] of the field [**hassadeh**] multiply [**veravvah**] against you [**alekha**].

**30** Little by little [**me'at me'at**] I will drive them out [**agareshennu**] from before you [**mippanekha**], until [**ad asher**] you are fruitful [**tiffreh**] and inherit [**venachalta**] the land [**et-ha'arets**].

**31** And I will set [**veshatti**] your border [**et-gevulekha**] from the Sea of [**miYam**] Reeds [**Suf**] to the Sea of [**ve'ad-Yam**] the Philistines [**Pelishtim**], and from the wilderness [**umimidbar**] to the River [**ad-hannahar**]; for I will give [**ki etten**] into your hand [**beyadkhem**] the inhabitants [**et yoshvey**] of the land [**ha'arets**], and you shall drive them out [**vegerashtamo**] from before you [**mippanekha**].

**32** You shall not make [**lo-tikhrot**] a covenant [**berit**] with them [**lahem**] or with their gods [**veloloheyhem**].

**33** They shall not dwell [**lo yeshvu**] in your land [**be'artsekha**], lest [**pen**] they cause you to sin [**yachati'u otekha**] against Me [**li**]; for if [**ki**] you serve [**ta'avod**] their gods [**et-eloheyhem**], it will surely be [**ki-yihyeh**] a snare [**lemoquesh**] to you [**lekha**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**shema shav** (v. 1) — "a false report, a rumor of emptiness." The noun shema ("report, hearing, what is heard") combined with shav ("emptiness, falsehood, vanity") — the same adjective that appears in the Decalogue's prohibition of "taking the name of YHWH your God in vain" (lashav, 20:7). The command not to "raise" (tissa) a false report uses the same verb (n-s-') as "you shall not take (tissa) the name of YHWH in vain." The verbal echo is surely deliberate: bearing false witness in the courtroom is as grave an offense against the covenant order as profaning the divine name. The LXX renders shema shav as akoēn mataian ("an empty/vain report"), with mataios capturing the sense of futility and moral emptiness in shav.

**lo tehdar** (v. 3) — "you shall not favor, you shall not show partiality to." The verb hadar typically means "to honor, adorn, show deference to" — used positively in Leviticus 19:32 ("you shall honor [tehadder] the face of an old man"). Here the command is strikingly countercultural: do not favor even the poor man (dal) in his legal dispute. Justice is not charity; the poor man's poverty does not make his case right. This verse stands in deliberate tension with v. 6 ("you shall not pervert the justice of your poor in his dispute"), which prohibits bias against the poor. Together vv. 3 and 6 form a frame: no partiality in either direction. The LXX renders this as ouk eleēseis penēta en krisei ("you shall not show mercy to a poor man in judgment"), using eleeo ("to have mercy/pity") — a term with stronger emotional resonance than the Hebrew hadar.

**hashev teshivennu** (v. 4) — "you shall surely return it." The Hiphil infinitive absolute with Hiphil imperfect of sh-u-v ("to return"). The emphatic construction signals an absolute obligation, not a discretionary kindness. The remarkable element is the context: the ox or donkey belongs to your enemy (oyivkha). The Torah here legislates against the natural human impulse to take satisfaction in an adversary's misfortune. You may not let your enemy's animal wander off and thereby increase his loss. The obligation to return the lost animal of an enemy is the Old Testament's closest parallel to the New Testament command to "love your enemies" (Matt 5:44), and it demonstrates that the ethic of enemy-love was not a Christian innovation but was already embedded in the Sinai covenant.

**azov ta'azov immo** (v. 5) — "you shall surely release it with him." The infinitive absolute with imperfect of '-z-v ("to leave, release, abandon"). The verse is syntactically difficult and has generated extensive commentary. The apparent sense is: when you see your enemy's donkey collapsed under its load, you must not "pass by" (chadal) but must help him unload the animal. The verb azav here has the unusual meaning "to release, set free" (i.e., release the donkey from its burden) rather than its more common meaning "to abandon." The LXX renders this verse with considerable expansion, clarifying the act of assistance. The ethical principle extends v. 4: not only must you return your enemy's stray animal, you must actively help your enemy when his beast collapses. The obligation is not passive non-malice but active beneficence.

**piqqechim** (v. 8) — "the clear-sighted, those with open eyes." From the root p-q-ch ("to open the eyes"), piqqeach denotes one who sees clearly — not physically but judicially and morally. The bribe "blinds the clear-sighted" (ye'aver piqqechim): even those who would otherwise see the truth plainly are blinded by the corrupting influence of payment. The parallel in Deuteronomy 16:19 uses chakkamim ("the wise") instead of piqqechim, showing the synonymous pairing: the bribe defeats both clear sight and wisdom. The LXX renders piqqechim as bleponton ("those who see"), a straightforward literal rendering. The choice of piqqechim rather than chakkamim may be deliberate: the emphasis is on perception rather than reasoning — the bribe does not make the judge stupid but makes him unable to see what is before his eyes.

**tishmetenah** (v. 11) — "you shall let it rest, you shall release it." The Qal imperfect of sh-m-t ("to release, let drop, let fall"). This is the verb from which the noun shemittah ("release") derives — the technical term for the Sabbath year. The command to "release" the land every seventh year is structurally parallel to the Sabbath command in v. 12 (rest every seventh day), creating a layered temporal architecture: weekly Sabbath, annual festivals (vv. 14-17), and now the septennial land-rest. The land itself is treated as a living participant in the covenant, entitled to its own sabbath. The produce of the fallow year belongs to the poor (evyoney ammekha, "the poor of your people") and after them to the wild animals — a remarkable ecological vision in which human agriculture yields periodically to the needs of the marginalized and of nature itself.

**shalosh regalim** (v. 14) — "three feet-journeys, three pilgrimage festivals." The noun regel means "foot," and regalim (plural) came to denote the pilgrimage festivals because they required foot-travel to the central sanctuary. The three regalim are Unleavened Bread (matstsot, v. 15), Harvest (qatsir, v. 16a, later called Shavuot/Weeks), and Ingathering (asif, v. 16b, later called Sukkot/Tabernacles). This is the earliest formulation of the pilgrimage calendar in the Torah; it will be expanded in Exodus 34:18-23, Deuteronomy 16:1-17, and Leviticus 23. The LXX renders regalim as kairous ("times, appointed occasions"), losing the pedestrian etymology but preserving the liturgical sense.

**lo-tevashel gedi bachalev immo** (v. 19b) — "you shall not boil a kid in the milk of its mother." This terse prohibition, repeated in Exodus 34:26 and Deuteronomy 14:21, became the foundation for the comprehensive rabbinic separation of meat and dairy (basar bechalav). In its original context, the prohibition likely targets a specific cultic practice — boiling a young goat in its mother's milk as a fertility rite or offering. A text from Ugarit (KTU 1.23, line 14) has been cited as evidence of such a Canaanite practice, though the reading is disputed. The juxtaposition with the festival calendar and the firstfruits offering suggests the prohibition's original setting is cultic rather than dietary. The LXX renders straightforwardly: ou hepsēseis arna en galakti mētros autou ("you shall not boil a lamb in its mother's milk"), substituting arna ("lamb") for the Hebrew gedi ("kid/young goat") — a notable lexical variant that may reflect a broader application of the law or a different Vorlage.

**mal'akh** (v. 20) — "angel, messenger." From the root l-'-kh ("to send"), the mal'akh is one who is sent on a mission. The angel of vv. 20-23 is one of the most theologically significant figures in the Torah. He is sent by God, yet God says "My name is in his midst" (shemi beqirbo, v. 21) — an extraordinary claim that places the divine identity within the messenger. He has the authority to forgive or withhold forgiveness for transgression (v. 21), a prerogative elsewhere reserved for God alone. The mal'akh is thus not a mere envoy but a bearer of divine presence and authority — a figure that later Jewish and Christian theology would interpret, respectively, as the Metatron (the "Angel of the Presence") or as a pre-incarnate theophany of the divine Word. The LXX renders mal'akh as angelos, the term that gives English its word "angel." The phrase shemi beqirbo is rendered to onoma mou estin ep' autō ("My name is upon him"), with ep' ("upon") rather than en ("in") — a slight but theologically notable shift from indwelling to investiture.

**hatsir'ah** (v. 28) — "the hornet." The noun tsir'ah (with the definite article ha-) denotes a stinging insect, most likely the hornet. Its mention here as a divine weapon of conquest is striking: YHWH will employ not only terror and confusion (v. 27) but also the natural world's smallest warriors. The same term appears in Deuteronomy 7:20 and Joshua 24:12. Whether the "hornet" is literal (swarms of stinging insects driving out populations) or metaphorical (panic, plague, or Egypt itself — the hornet being a symbol of Lower Egypt) has been debated since antiquity. The LXX renders hatsir'ah as tas sphēkias ("the wasps/hornets"), preserving the entomological reference. The theological point is clear in either reading: the conquest is YHWH's work, accomplished through instruments both great and small.

**me'at me'at** (v. 30) — "little by little." The reduplication of the adverb me'at ("a little") creates an expression of gradual, incremental progress. The divine strategy for the conquest is not sudden and total but measured and progressive, and the reason given is ecological and practical: a sudden depopulation of the land would leave it desolate (shemamah) and overrun by wild beasts (v. 29). This pragmatic divine reasoning is remarkable — God accommodates His timetable to the carrying capacity of the land. The principle of "little by little" became a significant theological concept in later Jewish interpretation: God's work in the world is often gradual rather than catastrophic, incremental rather than instantaneous.

### Grammatical Notes

**lo-tihyeh acharey-rabbim lera'ot** (v. 2) — "You shall not be after the many for evil." The syntax of v. 2 is notoriously difficult and has generated extensive debate. The MT reads literally: "You shall not be after the many for evil, and you shall not answer concerning a dispute to turn aside after the many to pervert." The verse appears to contain two prohibitions: (1) do not follow the crowd to do evil; (2) do not testify in a lawsuit in a way that bends toward the majority to distort justice. The verb lo-tihyeh ("you shall not be") is unusual — one expects lo-telekh ("you shall not go/follow"). The LXX renders the verse differently, reading it as a prohibition against joining with the many (meta plethous) to turn aside (ekklinein) toward the majority (meta pleionōn). The Masoretic accentuation suggests reading the verse as a single complex prohibition rather than two separate ones, but the formal equivalence translation preserves the apparent twofold structure.

**vechadalta me'azov lo** (v. 5) — The syntax is compressed and ambiguous. The verb chadal means "to cease, desist, refrain." Me'azov is the infinitive construct of azav with the preposition min. The phrase could mean: (a) "you shall refrain from abandoning it to him" (i.e., you shall not walk away); or (b) "shall you indeed refrain from releasing [the burden] with him?" (a rhetorical question expecting a negative answer). Most translations adopt reading (a), understanding the verse as a command to help. The LXX appears to interpret the verse as a positive command to assist, expanding the Hebrew considerably. The formal equivalence rendering follows the mainstream interpretation: you must not leave your enemy's collapsed donkey without helping.

**shamoa tishma beqolo** (v. 22) — "if you indeed listen to his voice." The infinitive absolute with imperfect of sh-m-' ("to hear, listen, obey"), followed by the prepositional phrase beqolo ("to/in his voice"). The emphatic construction marks the conditional protasis of a covenant promise: if you truly obey, then I will be an enemy to your enemies. The shift from the angel's voice (qolo, v. 22a) to "all that I speak" (kol asher adabber, v. 22b) further collapses the distinction between the angel and God — obeying the angel's voice is identical with obeying God's speech. The LXX renders this with ean akoē akousēte tēs emēs phōnēs ("if by hearing you hear My voice"), making explicit what the Hebrew leaves ambiguous: the voice is God's own voice.

**ve'ayyavti et-oyevekha vetsarti et-tsaraykha** (v. 22) — "I will be an enemy to your enemies and an adversary to your adversaries." The verbs are denominatives: 'ayyavti from oyev ("enemy") and tsarti from tsar ("adversary, oppressor"). The construction is a divine oath of solidarity: God identifies Himself as co-belligerent with Israel against Israel's foes. The chiastic structure — enemy/enemies, adversary/adversaries — emphasizes the completeness of the identification. The LXX renders this as echthreuō tois echthrois sou kai antikeisomai tois antikeimenois soi ("I will be hostile to those hostile to you and opposed to those opposed to you"), preserving the figure of polyptoton (the repetition of the same root in different forms).

### Structural Notes

Exodus 23 concludes the Book of the Covenant (sefer habberit) that began at 21:1, forming the final section of the legal corpus given to Moses at Sinai for the regulation of Israel's communal life. The chapter divides into four major sections, each with a distinct legal and theological character.

The first section (vv. 1-9) concerns justice and equity in legal proceedings. These are primarily apodictic commands ("you shall not...") addressed to the individual Israelite as participant in the judicial process — as witness (v. 1), as member of the assembly (v. 2), as judge (vv. 3, 6-8), and as neighbor of the vulnerable sojourner (v. 9). The section is framed by the sojourner-protection motif: v. 9 recalls the ger-command of 22:21, forming an inclusio around the judicial legislation. Within this frame, vv. 4-5 interrupt the court-law sequence with two case-laws about helping an enemy's animal — a striking intrusion of personal ethics into the judicial context, suggesting that justice is not only a matter of courtroom procedure but of daily conduct toward one's adversary.

The second section (vv. 10-13) establishes the sabbatical institutions: the seventh-year land-rest (vv. 10-11) and the seventh-day rest (v. 12), followed by a comprehensive warning against invoking the names of other gods (v. 13). The pairing of sabbath-year and sabbath-day creates a temporal architecture of covenant life: work and rest alternate in a divinely ordered rhythm at both the weekly and the septennial scale. The purpose clauses are notable: the land-rest is "for the poor of your people" and for wildlife (v. 11); the weekly rest is "in order that your ox and your donkey may rest, and the son of your female servant and the sojourner may be refreshed" (v. 12). The sabbath is thus not primarily a cultic institution but a social-justice institution — its primary beneficiaries are the vulnerable (animals, slaves, aliens).

The third section (vv. 14-19) establishes the three annual pilgrimage festivals: Unleavened Bread (matstsot), Harvest (qatsir), and Ingathering (asif). This is the most concise of the Torah's festival calendars (cf. Exod 34:18-23; Lev 23; Num 28-29; Deut 16). The agricultural orientation is conspicuous: the festivals are tied to the rhythm of planting, first-harvest, and final ingathering. The section concludes with the enigmatic prohibition against boiling a kid in its mother's milk (v. 19b), whose placement within the festival legislation suggests a cultic rather than purely dietary context.

The fourth and climactic section (vv. 20-33) shifts from legislation to promissory prophecy. YHWH announces the sending of His angel as guide and guardian for the journey to the promised land (vv. 20-23), commands the destruction of Canaanite cult objects (v. 24), promises blessings of sustenance, health, fertility, and military victory (vv. 25-28), and describes the gradual process of conquest (vv. 29-30). The section closes with a territorial description of the promised land (v. 31) and a final prohibition against covenants with the Canaanites and their gods (vv. 32-33). This section is the theological capstone of the entire Book of the Covenant: the laws of chapters 21-23 are not arbitrary regulations but the terms of life in the land that God is about to give. Obedience brings blessing and possession; disobedience brings the "snare" (moqesh, v. 33) of idolatry and the loss of everything promised.

### Theological Notes

The justice-laws of vv. 1-9 articulate a vision of judicial integrity that is remarkable for its comprehensiveness and its refusal of easy categories. The prohibition against favoring the poor (v. 3) stands alongside the prohibition against perverting the poor man's justice (v. 6) — together they demand a justice that is blind to economic status in either direction. The prohibition against following the majority to do evil (v. 2) strikes at the deepest root of judicial corruption: the human tendency to conform to group pressure rather than stand for truth. The command to help an enemy's collapsed donkey (v. 5) extends justice beyond the courtroom into daily life and beyond the circle of friends into the territory of adversaries. The theological grounding of the entire section is the sojourner-command of v. 9, with its appeal to Israel's own experience of oppression: "you know the soul of the sojourner, for sojourners you were in the land of Egypt." The word nefesh ("soul") is significant — Israel does not merely know the external conditions of alienation but knows its inner experience, its psychic reality. Empathy born of memory is the moral foundation of justice.

The angel of vv. 20-23 is one of the most theologically dense figures in the entire Pentateuch. The statement "My name is in his midst" (shemi beqirbo) places the angel in a category unlike any other messenger in Scripture. The "name" (shem) of God is not a label but a revelation of divine character and presence — it is the functional equivalent of God's own self. To say that the divine name dwells "in the midst of" the angel is to say that the angel bears the divine presence in a uniquely intimate way. The angel can forgive sin (or withhold forgiveness) — a divine prerogative (cf. Isa 43:25; Mark 2:7). The angel's voice is God's voice (v. 22). Yet the angel is clearly sent by God and is therefore distinct from God. This irreducible tension between identity and distinction in the mal'akh YHWH became a generative source for later theological reflection on divine mediation — in the Targumim's Memra, in Philo's Logos, and in Christian Christology.

The gradual-conquest motif (vv. 29-30) contains a profound theological insight: God's purposes are accomplished not instantaneously but progressively, and the reason for the delay is not divine weakness but divine wisdom. A sudden, total conquest would leave the land desolate and overrun with wild animals — the ecological consequence of depopulation. God therefore works "little by little" (me'at me'at), accommodating His timetable to the realities of the created order. This principle — that divine action respects the constraints of creation rather than overriding them — stands in tension with the more dramatic images of divine intervention elsewhere in Exodus (the plagues, the sea-crossing). The Torah thus presents two complementary modes of divine action: the catastrophic (for liberation from Egypt) and the incremental (for settlement in Canaan). Both are expressions of the same sovereign will, adapted to different circumstances.

The concluding warning against covenant with the Canaanites (vv. 32-33) brings the Book of the Covenant to a close with the ultimate threat: that serving other gods will become a "snare" (moqesh) — a trapper's device that catches the unwary. The metaphor is revealing: idolatry is not presented as a frontal assault but as a hidden trap. The danger is not that Israel will consciously choose to abandon YHWH but that coexistence and covenant-making with the Canaanites will gradually, imperceptibly draw Israel into the worship of other gods. The word moqesh, from the root y-q-sh ("to lay a snare"), conveys the insidious, concealed character of the threat. The entire Book of the Covenant, which began with laws about slaves and injuries and moved through property, justice, sabbath, and festival, ends with this stark warning: the greatest danger to Israel is not external enemies but internal seduction. The laws are given not as burdens but as safeguards — the framework within which covenantal faithfulness can be sustained and the snare avoided.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 23 (pages 65-66) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 2, the LXX clarifies the notoriously difficult Hebrew syntax with a more transparent prohibition against siding with the majority to pervert justice (ou prosthēsē meta plethous ekklinai krisin, "you shall not join with the many to turn aside justice"). At v. 3, the MT's dal ("poor, weak") is rendered penēta ("poor man"), and the verb tehdar ("favor, honor") is rendered eleēseis ("show pity/mercy") — a more emotionally charged term that shifts the prohibition from deference to compassion. At v. 5, the LXX significantly expands the compressed Hebrew, making the obligation to assist the enemy's fallen donkey more explicit and unambiguous. At v. 8, the MT's piqqechim ("the clear-sighted") is rendered bleponton ("those who see"), a straightforward translation. At v. 11, the sabbath-year legislation is rendered with aphesis ("release"), the same term used for the Jubilee and for the "release" of debts. At v. 17, the MT's ha'Adon YHWH ("the Lord YHWH") is rendered kuriou tou theou sou ("the Lord your God"), with the characteristic Septuagintal substitution of kurios for YHWH and the addition of "your God." At v. 19, the LXX substitutes arna ("lamb") for the MT's gedi ("kid/young goat") in the prohibition against boiling in mother's milk — a notable variant that may reflect a broader interpretation of the prohibition or a different Hebrew text. At v. 20, the mal'akh ("angel") is rendered angelos, and the critical phrase shemi beqirbo ("My name is in his midst") is rendered to onoma mou estin ep' autō ("My name is upon him"), with the preposition ep' ("upon") rather than en ("in") — a subtle theological difference between external investiture and internal indwelling. At v. 21, the statement "he will not forgive your transgression" is rendered ou mē huposteilētai se ("he will not draw back from you / will not spare you"), a different nuance that emphasizes the angel's severity rather than his judicial authority. At v. 28, hatsir'ah ("the hornet") is rendered tas sphēkias ("the wasps"), preserving the insect reference. Throughout the Vaticanus pages, the text of Exodus 23 begins in the lower portion of page 65 (following the end of chapter 22, which the LXX numbers differently) and continues through the upper and middle portions of page 66, where it transitions into chapter 24. The text is well-preserved in this section with clear, legible uncial script in three columns.

--- LOGOS
